title: Green vs red: who won the slice?

diagnosticQuestions.prompt: In the usual color setup, a “bullish” candle often means:
choices: Close was below open (sellers pushed harder)
Close was above open (buyers won that slice)
diagnosticQuestions.hint: Bulls like when price finishes higher than it started in that window.

diagnosticQuestions.prompt: A long upper wick can suggest (in simple words):
choices: Price tried to go higher but was pushed back down before the close
diagnosticQuestions.hint: The tip shows a high that couldn’t be held by the close.

teachSlides.title: Two teams in one bar
teachSlides.body: Think of buyers and sellers arguing during that time slice. If the close is above the open, buyers were stronger in the end — often colored green/white (bullish). If the close is below the open, sellers had the edge — often red (bearish).

teachSlides.title: Wicks tell drama
teachSlides.body: Long wicks mean price went somewhere dramatic during the slice, then snapped back. Upper wick: tried higher, failed to hold. Lower wick: dipped, then bounced.

teachSlides.title: Patterns are hints, not promises
teachSlides.body: Names like “hammer” or “engulfing” are shorthand for shapes. They describe mood in that window — not a guarantee of the next window.

recapSlide.title: Recap: color and wicks
recapSlide.body: Bullish slice: close > open (often green). Bearish: close < open (often red). Long wicks show rejection or recovery within the bar. Names describe shape; risk rules still matter.

masteryQuestions.prompt: A bullish candle in the common coloring means:
masteryQuestions.explanation: Buyers finished stronger than where the slice started.

masteryQuestions.prompt: A long upper wick mainly highlights:
choices: That intraday highs were tested but not held by the close
masteryQuestions.explanation: The session probed higher, then price fell back from that extreme.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Pattern names on charts are best treated as:
choices: Descriptive labels that still need context and risk management
masteryQuestions.explanation: They summarize shape; edge comes from process, not memorizing labels alone.
